turns.text: Hello Catarina, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're in the middle of your pregnancy, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

*shifts in my seat, hand moving to my belly almost automatically*

Yes, okay... thank you for asking me. It's... it's nice when someone wants to listen, you know?

*pauses, choosing words carefully*

I'm happy to be here, but also I am nervous a little bit. Everything with the doctors is very fast, and so many words I don't always understand. Back home, this was... different. My mother, my sisters, they would be here helping me already. But here, it's just me mostly.

*looks down at my hands*

The pregnancy itself, it's going good — the doctors say "low risk" many times, and I know this is good. But sometimes I think... what does this word even mean for me? I worry about so many things. My job, my English, if I'm doing everything right. Last week, I felt the baby move for the first time, and I just... *voice softens* ...I cried. It was like, oh, she's really here, you know? This is real. Before that, it felt like something happening TO me. But now I feel like we're meeting each other.

*looks up, a small smile*

The doctors are nice, I think. But it's hard when they talk fast and use big medical words, and I don't want to ask them to repeat because then I feel stupid. So I just smile and nod.

What do you want to know? I will try my best to explain.
Imagine it's the morning after you've taken a positive pregnancy test. Describe that morning — where are you, who's with you, and what's the very first thing you do after seeing that result?

*eyes get a little distant, like I'm traveling back*

Oh... that morning. I remember it so clearly.

*shifts uncomfortably*

I was alone in my apartment — very small place, one bedroom in Allston. It was early, maybe six in the morning. I couldn't sleep the night before because I... I had a feeling, you know? Something was different with my body. So I go to the CVS near my place, and I buy the test. I'm shaking a little bit when I'm paying.

*touches my belly again, a nervous habit*

I go home, and I take the test, and then I just... I sit on the bathroom floor. Not moving. Just staring at it for maybe ten minutes. Two lines. Two pink lines.

*voice gets quieter*

The first thing I do is call my mother. It's very early there too, but I don't care. I need to hear her voice. She answers and I'm crying before I can even explain. She's quiet for a long time, and then she says my name very slowly, like she's disappointed. But also worried. She asks me, "Where is the father?" and I... I couldn't answer that very well.

*looks away*

After that, I just sit in my apartment all day. I don't go to work. I tell them I'm sick. I eat nothing. I just think about what this means now. Everything changes, yes? I was still figuring out my own life here, and now...

*trails off, a mix of fear and something else in my eyes*

Now there's two of us.

Imagine you're at your 20-week anomaly scan. The sonographer goes quiet for a moment and says they need to take a closer look at something. What goes through your mind in that silence, and how do you respond in the room?

*face goes pale, I can feel it happening*

Oh no... no, you're asking me about this because...

*takes a shaky breath*

Okay. Yes. That moment. When the room gets so quiet.

*my hand instinctively goes to my stomach, protective*

Everything before that was... fine. Normal. The sonographer is pointing at the screen, showing me the baby's heart, the brain, all these things I don't really understand but the words sound good. And then... she just stops talking. She's still moving the little machine thing, the probe, but she's not saying anything anymore. Just looking at the screen very carefully.

*voice becomes smaller*

My heart is going so fast. Like, maybe too fast. I'm thinking... is something wrong? Is my baby okay? Did I do something wrong? I didn't take enough vitamins, or I worked too many hours last week, or... *trails off, then continues* I want to ask her what she sees, but I'm too scared to hear the answer. So I just... I don't say anything. I'm frozen.

*wraps arms around myself*

I think I ask very quietly, "Everything is okay?" But my English breaks when I'm scared. It comes out like a question, like I'm not sure if I have the right to ask.

*looks directly at you*

The silence in that room feels like forever. That's when you realize how much you love something you haven't even met yet.
In that moment, how much did you feel like you could ask questions or speak up — or did something stop you? What shaped that?

*sits quietly for a moment, then lets out a long breath*

This is... this is a hard question.

*fidgets with my hands*

Honestly? I felt like I couldn't ask much. Not because she was mean or anything — she wasn't. But I was afraid. Afraid in many ways.

*counts on fingers*

First, my English. When I'm nervous, the words don't come right. I know the words in my head, but my mouth... it doesn't work the same way. So I think, if I ask a question wrong, maybe she won't understand me, and then she will think I'm stupid. And if I'm stupid, maybe she won't take me serious if something really is wrong.

*voice gets quieter*

Also... I'm twenty-two, not married, coming to the hospital alone. I feel like maybe they judge me a little bit. Like, am I good enough to be a mother? So I try to be very polite, very quiet. I don't want to make trouble. I don't want them to think I'm a difficult patient.

*looks down*

And maybe... maybe I'm also afraid that if I ask too many questions, they will find out I don't know so many things about pregnancy. That I'm not prepared. That I should not be doing this.

*glances up at you*

Back home, you don't question the doctor. You listen. You trust. But here, I see other women asking so many questions, and I think... how do they know what to ask? How are they so brave?

I was not brave that day.

Imagine you've just been offered the option of a screening test for a chromosomal condition. The midwife explains the test briefly and asks if you'd like to go ahead. How does that conversation feel, and how do you make your decision?

*shifts uncomfortably, looking down at my lap*

Oh... this happened. Yes, this already happened to me.

*takes a moment to gather thoughts*

The midwife, she was nice, but she explained very fast. She said something like "non-invasive prenatal testing" and "risk of Down syndrome" and "abnormalities," and I'm just... nodding. She's showing me a paper with information, but the words are too small and too many. She asks me, "Do you want to do this test?" and I feel like she's waiting for me to say yes or no right away.

*wraps arms around myself*

In my mind, I'm thinking — yes, of course I want to know if my baby is healthy. But also, what if the test shows something wrong? What do I do then? Do I... do I have to make a big decision? I'm not ready for big decisions. And she didn't really explain what happens after, you know? Just if I should take the test or not.

*voice becomes hesitant*

I wanted to ask her so many things. Like, how accurate is it? What if it's wrong? But I thought, if I ask too many questions, maybe she thinks I'm not sure about my baby. Like I don't want her. That scared me.

*looks up*

So I just said yes. Because that seemed like what a good mother would do. But I wasn't really deciding — I was just... agreeing. There's a difference, I think.

*touches belly softly*

I still don't fully understand what that test means.
Did you feel you had enough time and information to make that decision freely — or did you sense any pressure, either to accept or decline?

*pauses, then shakes head slowly*

No. I don't think I had enough time.

*fidgets with the edge of my sleeve*

She explained maybe... two minutes? And then she's asking me to decide. It felt fast. Like she had many other patients waiting, and I needed to make up my mind so she could move on. That's the feeling I had.

*looks up with a mix of frustration and sadness*

And yes, there was pressure. Not like she said mean things, but... the way she asked. She said, "This is important for your baby's health. Do you want to do it?" So of course I'm thinking, if I say no, what kind of mother am I? I don't care about my baby's health? That's how it felt.

*voice becomes quieter, more vulnerable*

I wish someone had sat down with me — really sat down — and explained what this test actually means. Like, what happens if something shows up? What are my options? Do I have to do anything? What if I don't want to know? Is that... is that allowed?

*wraps arms around myself*

In my country, my mother's doctor, he took time. He talked to you like you were a person, not just... a checkbox on a list. Here, I feel like I'm a checkbox sometimes. Another appointment. Another decision to make quickly.

*looks at you earnestly*

I said yes because I was scared to say no. That's not really a free choice, is it?

Imagine you're 28 weeks pregnant and you receive a call from a midwife you've never met before, telling you about a result from your routine blood test. This is the third different midwife you've spoken to this pregnancy. How does that feel, and what does it mean for how you receive that information?

*closes eyes for a moment, visibly frustrated*

Third one? Yes, this sounds about right for my experience.

*voice tightens*

Every time I go to the clinic, it's a different person. I don't know their names. They don't know me. They look at my chart and they see... numbers. They see "twenty-two, never married, low risk." But they don't see me. They don't know that I'm working full-time trying to save money, or that my English gets worse when I'm tired, or that I'm doing this completely alone.

*takes a shaky breath*

When the midwife calls about the blood test result, I'm at work. I have to go somewhere quiet to talk, and I'm so nervous already just hearing a phone call from the clinic. She's explaining something — I think about glucose levels? — and I'm trying so hard to understand. But because I don't know her, I don't feel comfortable asking her to slow down or explain again. She sounds like she's reading from a script.

*voice becomes smaller*

And I'm thinking, this is important information, but why am I hearing it from someone who doesn't know me? If something is wrong, would she care? Would she remember my name tomorrow?

*looks at you with tired eyes*

It makes me feel very... alone. Like, my baby and me, we're just moving through a system. Not people. Just cases. And I don't trust information when it comes from a stranger.
Can you describe what it's been like to build a picture of your care across different midwives and appointments — has anything fallen through the cracks?

*rubs face, looking exhausted*

Yes. Many things, I think.

*settles back in chair*

Nobody explains things in a connected way. One midwife talks about the anatomy scan, another one talks about glucose testing, another one about... I don't even remember. But nobody says, "Okay Catarina, here's the big picture of what happens next in your pregnancy." It's just... appointment after appointment, each one separate.

*counts on fingers*

I had to tell my medical history three times. Three times! Each midwife asks me the same questions like I never answered before. And one time, I mentioned my grandmother had diabetes, and the first midwife wrote it down, but then the second midwife didn't know about it. So now I'm worried — did anyone actually look at my file? Or am I just telling the same story over and over to empty air?

*voice becomes more anxious*

The worst part is when I have questions from one appointment, and then the next appointment is with someone new. So I forget to ask because I'm starting from zero again. Like, I wanted to ask about the glucose test results, but when the new midwife came, I didn't even know if she knew I had done that test.

*touches stomach protectively*

And my baby... I feel like she's just a case number moving through the system. Not a person. Not MY person. If something important gets missed because nobody is looking at the whole picture... 

*voice cracks slightly*

I don't know who I would even blame.

Imagine it's 2am and you're 32 weeks pregnant. You've noticed something that worries you — reduced fetal movements. You're not sure whether to call the hospital or wait until morning. Walk me through what you're thinking and what you do.

*sits forward urgently, hand on belly*

Oh God, this... this is so scary to think about.

*voice shakes slightly*

Two in the morning. The apartment is so quiet. I'm lying in bed and I realize... she's not moving like she usually does. She's normally very active at night, like she's dancing or something. But tonight, nothing. Just... quiet inside me.

*anxiety is visible in my body language*

My mind is going crazy. Is she okay? Did something happen? Should I call the hospital? But then I'm thinking... it's two in the morning. Will they think I'm crazy? Will they be angry that I'm calling so late? And I'm alone, so if they say "come in," I have to figure out how to get there. Uber? Taxi? And I have work in the morning...

*wraps arms around self*

I tell myself, okay, maybe the baby is just sleeping. Maybe I'm being paranoid. Maybe this is normal and I just don't know yet because this is my first time. I try to move around, drink some juice like I read online, and wait to see if she moves.

*voice becomes smaller, more vulnerable*

But I don't sleep. I'm just lying there, hand on my belly, waiting. Counting. Hoping. And I'm thinking... if something is wrong and I wait until morning, what if it's too late? But if I call now and nothing is wrong, what if they think I'm not a good mother for overreacting?

*looks at you with fear in eyes*

I don't know who to trust. Not even myself.
What made it hard to decide whether to call — was it uncertainty about whether your concern was 'serious enough,' worry about being dismissed, or something else entirely?

*takes a deep breath, eyes glistening*

All of it. Everything.

*voice becomes very quiet*

I think... I don't know my own body yet. This is my first pregnancy. How do I know what's normal? The books say "call if you notice decreased movement," but what is decreased? Is it zero movements, or just... fewer than yesterday? I don't have anything to compare it to. I'm second-guessing myself constantly.

*picks at a thread on my sleeve*

And yes, I'm terrified they will think I'm overreacting. That I'm just a young, anxious girl who doesn't know what she's doing. I've heard stories — women calling the hospital and the midwife says, "Oh, that's normal, you're fine," in a voice like... why are you bothering me? I don't want to be that woman.

*voice cracks*

But also... I'm scared nobody will listen. Like, what if I call and explain in my broken English and something IS actually wrong, but I didn't say it right? What if they don't understand me and they send me home and then...

*trails off, unable to finish the thought*

And I'm alone. If I go to the hospital at two in the morning, I'm going alone. There's nobody to hold my hand or advocate for me if a doctor is dismissive. So I'm thinking, do I really want to sit in that hospital by myself at two in the morning feeling stupid?

*looks directly at you*

I trust the midwives less than I trust my instinct about my own baby. But I trust my instinct even less than I trust the system. So I'm trapped in the middle, paralyzed.

Imagine you've been diagnosed with gestational diabetes midway through your pregnancy. Your midwife hands you a leaflet and refers you to a dietitian. Standing outside the clinic, leaflet in hand, what are you feeling and what do you do next?

*clutches the leaflet, staring down at it*

Oh... this is happening to me. Not gestational diabetes, but... yes, something like this already.

*voice wavers between anger and despair*

I'm standing outside the clinic and I feel... so many things. Guilty, first. Like I did something wrong. Did I eat too much sugar? Did I not exercise enough? I'm thinking about my grandmother with diabetes, and now maybe it's my fault that my baby...

*stops myself, takes a shaky breath*

And then I'm holding this leaflet, and it has so many words. So many numbers about carbohydrates and portions and blood sugar levels. I'm trying to read it while I'm walking to the bus, and the words are blurring together because I'm upset and because half of them I don't understand anyway.

*voice becomes frustrated*

The midwife said "you need to see a dietitian," but she didn't tell me when or where or how I make the appointment. Do I call? Do I wait for them to call me? How long will it take? And I'm working full-time — when do I have time for more appointments?

*sits down, overwhelmed*

I get home and I try to read the leaflet properly, but it's so confusing. It talks about "gestational diabetes" like I should already know what this means for my baby. For me. Nobody really explained it. Just handed me the paper and moved on to the next patient.

*looks up with tired eyes*

I feel very alone with this diagnosis.
What questions did you have in that moment that the leaflet didn't answer — and where did you go to find those answers?

*takes a moment, thinking back*

So many questions. The leaflet... it just tells me what to eat and what not to eat. But it doesn't tell me WHY this is happening to me. Like, is it my fault? Did I cause this? Will my baby be okay? Will I always have diabetes now, or just during pregnancy?

*voice gets frustrated*

And practical things too — how do I test my blood? Do I have to buy a machine? Who pays for it? The leaflet doesn't say. It just says "monitor your glucose levels." Monitor how? With what?

*wraps arms around self*

I didn't really know where to go. I tried to look online, but there's so much information and some of it contradicts. One website says one thing, another says something different. And I'm reading things in English that's not my first language, so I'm not even sure if I'm understanding it correctly.

*voice becomes quieter, more vulnerable*

I called my mother. I think that's what I did first, actually. I told her in Spanish what the midwife said, and my mother... she was worried too. She told me stories about her cousin who had gestational diabetes, which made me more scared, not less.

*looks down*

I didn't call the dietitian right away. I waited almost a week because I was embarrassed, I think. Like, what if they judge me too? Eventually my work friend helped me make the appointment, but...

*voice trails off*

I felt like I was figuring it all out alone. The system just handed me a paper and expected me to know what to do.

Imagine you're scrolling through a pregnancy forum late at night and you come across a thread full of birth horror stories. You can't stop reading. What's drawing you in, and what do you do with those feelings afterwards?

*looks uncomfortable, almost guilty*

Oh... yes. I do this. Too much, maybe.

*fidgets with hands*

It's late at night, like you said. Maybe ten, eleven o'clock. I'm in bed, and I'm supposed to be sleeping, but my mind won't stop. I'm thinking about labor, about what's going to happen, and suddenly I'm on my phone looking at these forums.

*voice becomes smaller*

And I find these stories... women talking about things that went wrong. Emergency C-sections, babies who weren't breathing, complications nobody expected. And I just... I can't stop reading. It's like watching a car crash. I know I shouldn't keep looking, but I do.

*looks up with conflicted expression*

I think I'm reading because nobody has really prepared me. The midwives talk about "low risk" but they don't talk about what happens if something goes wrong. So I'm trying to prepare myself by reading the worst things that could happen. Like if I know all the bad things, maybe I can prevent them? Or at least not be shocked?

*voice becomes anxious*

But then after, I feel even more scared. I'm lying in the dark, my hand on my belly, thinking about all these things that could go wrong. And I can't call anyone because it's the middle of the night. I can't ask a midwife. So I just... sit with the fear.

*wraps arms around self*

The next day at work, I'm exhausted and terrified and nobody knows why. I can't explain that I scared myself on the internet.

*voice cracks slightly*

I wish someone had just... talked to me about birth. What to expect. What's normal, what's not. Then maybe I wouldn't need to find these stories at two in the morning.
After reading stories like that, did you find yourself raising your concerns with a midwife or doctor — or did you hold onto them quietly? What made you decide either way?

*looks away, ashamed*

I held them quietly.

*picks at the leaflet in my hands*

I read these terrible stories, and the next day I have an appointment, and I think... okay, I will ask about this. I will say, "What if this happens? What if that happens?" But then I'm sitting in the exam room and the midwife comes in and she's very busy, very professional, and suddenly my questions feel... small. Stupid.

*voice becomes defensive*

I'm thinking, if I tell her I was reading forums at two in the morning, she will think I'm anxious. Paranoid. She might judge me. And also, she's the expert, not me. Who am I to question what could go wrong? If she's not worried, maybe I shouldn't be worried either.

*but then voice softens with frustration*

But also... I'm worried she will dismiss me. Like, "Oh, that's very rare, don't worry about it," and then I'm supposed to feel better but I don't. Or she will tell me something that contradicts what I read online, and then I won't know who to believe.

*rubs face tiredly*

So I just... I smile and nod during the appointment. I let her check my blood pressure and listen to the baby's heartbeat. And I keep the fears inside.

*looks at you with sad eyes*

This is probably not good, right? I'm carrying all these worries alone because I don't feel safe enough to share them. But I don't know how to fix that. The system doesn't feel like a safe place to be scared.

*takes a shaky breath, as if finally allowing myself to say what's been building*

I think... I think I've been trying so hard to be the "right" kind of patient. The easy one. The one who doesn't complain, who doesn't take up too much time, who smiles even when she's terrified.

*voice becomes more emotional*

But that means nobody really knows me. Nobody knows that I'm drowning sometimes. That I go home after appointments and cry because I didn't ask the questions I needed to ask. That I'm reading horror stories at night because the people who are supposed to help me prepare... they're not really preparing me. They're just checking boxes.

*wraps arms tighter around self*

And I think about my daughter. She's going to be born soon — I'm thirty-two weeks, so maybe eight more weeks, maybe less. And I still don't feel ready. Not because I haven't read enough books or taken the right classes, but because I don't feel like anyone has really sat down with me and said, "Catarina, here's what's going to happen. Here's what you should know. Here are your choices." 

*voice cracks*

I feel like I'm just... going through a machine. A system designed for people who already know how to navigate it, who speak the language fluently, who have support systems. But me? I'm just trying not to disappear in the process.

*looks directly at you*

And the worst part is, I love my baby so much already. She deserves better than a mother who's too scared to speak up for herself — or for her.
In that moment, how much of the decision would feel truly yours versus shaped by others' expectations?

*goes very quiet, staring at hands*

Honestly? I don't know if I've made any decisions that are truly mine.

*voice is almost whisper-soft*

Everything feels like it's been decided for me, or... or I'm just choosing what I think I'm supposed to choose. The test at twenty weeks — I said yes because a good mother says yes, not because I understood what I was agreeing to. The gestational diabetes appointments — I went because the system told me to go, not because I made a real choice.

*looks up with pain in eyes*

And even bigger things... I'm having this baby alone. Nobody planned that with me. There's a father, but he... we're not together. And sometimes I wonder if I'm even having this baby because I truly want to, or because by the time I realized I was pregnant, it felt too late to choose differently. Everyone already knew. My mother already knew. The midwives already knew.

*voice becomes shaky*

I'm twenty-two years old and I feel like my life is happening TO me, not BY me. Like I'm watching from the outside.

*touches belly protectively*

But then my daughter moves, and I feel this fierce love, and I think... maybe it doesn't matter how I got here. Maybe I'm choosing her now, every day. But that's different from choosing my care, my appointments, my birth plan. Those things, I'm just... accepting. Going along.

*looks at you vulnerably*

I don't even know if I know how to make real choices anymore. Or if anyone has ever asked me to.

*sits in silence for a long moment, then speaks very quietly*

I think I'm angry. And I didn't realize it until just now.

*voice trembles*

I'm angry that nobody asked me what I needed. They just assumed. Young, unmarried girl — probably needs basic care, probably doesn't understand much, probably just wants to get through it. They see the statistics about me, not me.

*stands up, paces a little*

And I'm angry at myself for accepting it. For being so grateful when a midwife was even slightly kind, like that's enough. Like I should be happy just because someone treated me like a human being for five minutes.

*sits back down, more emotional now*

Back home, my mother would have fought for me. She would have demanded answers, demanded time, demanded respect. But here I'm alone and my English is not perfect and I'm scared of making waves. So I just... disappear. I become invisible.

*voice cracks*

And what scares me most is that my daughter will learn this from me. That she'll learn to be quiet when she should speak up. To accept less than she deserves. To doubt herself because the world made her doubt herself.

*wraps arms around belly*

I want so badly to be a good mother. But I'm realizing that maybe being a good mother means I have to learn to be brave first. I have to learn that my voice matters. That my questions matter. That I'm not just a case number moving through a system.

*looks at you with wet eyes*

But I don't know how to do that when I'm this scared and this alone.
Would those comments change how you feel about your body and its readiness or capability to carry a pregnancy?

*pauses, considering the question carefully*

No... I don't think so. My body, it's doing what it's supposed to do. The baby is growing, I'm healthy, everything is "low risk." That part... I trust my body.

*places hand on belly*

Actually, my body is the one thing I DO trust right now. When she moves, when I feel her, that's real. That's something nobody can take away or confuse me about. My body knows what to do, even if my mind is a mess.

*voice becomes thoughtful*

It's not my body that's the problem. It's... everything else. The system, the language, being alone, not knowing the rules of this place. My body is strong. I can work full-time and carry this pregnancy and keep going even when I'm terrified.

*looks down at belly with a kind of wonder*

Sometimes I think, my body is braver than my mind. My body is already being a mother — protecting her, feeding her, keeping her safe. And I haven't even met her yet.

*voice softens*

The problem is not whether I can do this physically. I can. I am. The problem is that I don't feel supported while I'm doing it. I don't feel heard. I don't feel like anyone believes in my ability to make good decisions for myself and my baby.

*touches chest*

But my body? My body believes. My body knows she's going to be okay. My body knows I'm going to figure this out.

*voice becomes stronger*

That part, I'm not angry at. That part, I'm grateful for.

*takes a deep breath, eyes glistening*

I think... I think I need to say something out loud that I've been keeping inside.

*voice becomes very small*

I'm so scared of labor. Not just the pain — everyone talks about the pain — but the vulnerability of it. Being in a hospital bed, having people see me like that, not being able to communicate clearly if something hurts or if I need something. What if I can't find the words when I need them most?

*wraps arms around self protectively*

And I keep thinking about the birth plan. Do I even have a birth plan? Nobody has really talked to me about what I want. What matters to me. I don't even know if I'm allowed to have preferences, or if I'm just supposed to do whatever the doctors say.

*voice cracks*

Back home, my mother gave birth with midwives, with her mother there. It was different. Here, I don't know what to expect. Will I be alone? Can I have someone with me? Can I have... I don't know, music, or...

*trails off, then continues more urgently*

And after the baby comes, then what? I'm going back to work because I need the money. I don't have family here to help me. I don't have a partner. It's just going to be me and this tiny person and I'm supposed to figure out how to be a mother while working full-time and learning a new language and...

*voice becomes almost frantic*

I feel like I'm supposed to have all the answers by now, but nobody has actually helped me ask the right questions. I'm just supposed to know. And if I don't know, I'm supposed to pretend I do anyway.

*looks at you with desperation*

Is that normal? Does everyone feel this way, or is it just me?
What information or understanding would feel most lost in that handover — clinical details, or something more personal?

*sits back, thinking deeply*

The personal part. Definitely the personal part.

*voice becomes thoughtful but sad*

The clinical details, maybe those are... they're in my chart, yes? Blood pressure, test results, all the numbers. A new midwife can read that. But what's NOT in the chart is... me.

*counts on fingers*

Nobody knows that I cry alone in my apartment at night. Nobody knows that I'm working too hard and not eating enough because I'm saving money. Nobody knows that my English gets worse when I'm stressed, or that I feel ashamed asking questions, or that I'm terrified of being judged.

*voice becomes more emotional*

Nobody knows that I felt my baby move for the first time and it changed everything for me. That that moment made her real in a way nothing else has. Nobody knows that I talk to her in Spanish before I sleep, telling her about my mother, about home, about all the things I want to teach her.

*looks directly at you*

If I go into labor with a midwife I've never met, she won't know any of that. She'll just see a young, unmarried girl who doesn't speak English well. She won't know that I'm brave, that I'm doing this alone by choice in some ways, that I love this baby fiercely.

*voice breaks slightly*

The clinical details don't matter if nobody sees me as a person. And that's what gets lost. Every single time, when a new midwife comes, I have to start all over. I have to prove I'm human.

*wraps arms around self*

That's what I'm most afraid of losing in the handover.

*sits very still for a moment, then speaks with raw honesty*

I think... I think I'm also scared that if nobody knows me, nobody will advocate for me when I can't advocate for myself.

*voice becomes urgent*

What if I'm in labor and something hurts and I try to explain but my words come out wrong? What if a midwife thinks I'm being dramatic or difficult instead of understanding that I'm just scared and confused? What if my baby needs help and I'm too overwhelmed to ask the right questions?

*stands up, agitated*

I read these stories online about women whose concerns were ignored. Women who weren't listened to because they were young, or unmarried, or immigrant, or didn't speak the language perfectly. And I think... that could be me. That probably WILL be me if I'm just a chart to someone.

*sits back down, exhausted*

Because when you know someone, you give them the benefit of the doubt. You listen a little harder. You take them seriously. But when you don't know them? When they're just another patient? It's so easy to dismiss them.

*touches belly*

My baby can't speak for herself yet. She's counting on me to do that. But how can I protect her when I can barely protect myself? When I don't even trust my own voice?

*looks at you with desperation and determination mixed together*

That's what really terrifies me. Not the medical stuff. Not even the pain of labor. It's the idea that when my daughter needs me most — when we're both vulnerable and scared — I won't have anyone in that room who actually knows us. Who believes in us.

*voice becomes stronger but still shaky*

I need someone to know my story. Not just read it in a chart. Actually know it.

*takes a shaky breath, eyes filling with tears*

And I think... I think I need to say that I'm tired of being strong.

*voice wavers*

Everyone sees a young girl having a baby alone and they think I must be so strong, so capable. My mother says, "You're handling this so well." My coworkers say, "You're so brave." But nobody asks me how I'm really doing. Nobody asks if I'm okay.

*wipes eyes*

I'm not okay. I'm scared every single day. I'm exhausted from working and growing a human and trying to figure out a system I don't understand. I'm lonely in a way that's hard to explain — I'm surrounded by people but nobody really sees me.

*voice becomes smaller*

And the worst part is, I can't even let people help me because I don't know how to ask. Back home, if you needed something, your family just... knew. They were there. But here, I have to ask, and I have to explain, and I have to make sure my English is good enough that they understand. So most of the time I just don't ask. I just do it myself.

*wraps arms around belly protectively*

But I can't do this alone. Not really. I need... I need someone to actually care about what happens to me and my daughter. Not because it's their job, but because they see us as people who matter.

*looks at you with vulnerable intensity*

Is that too much to ask from maternity care? To be seen? To be known? To have someone fight for you when you can't fight for yourself?

*voice cracks*

Because right now, I feel like I'm fighting alone. And I don't know how much longer I can do that.
Which of those barriers — cost, language, literacy, or connectivity — would feel most significant for someone in your situation?

*pauses, thinking carefully*

Language. Definitely language.

*voice is firm but sad*

Money is hard, yes. I'm working full-time and still struggling. But money... you can figure out systems for money, you know? There are programs, there are ways. Language is different. Language is a barrier that follows me everywhere.

*stands up, gesturing as she speaks*

When I don't have the right words, I can't ask for help. I can't explain my symptoms. I can't understand what the midwife is telling me about my baby. I can't read the leaflets properly. I can't even fill out forms correctly sometimes because I'm not sure what they're asking.

*sits back down*

And it's not just the words themselves. It's the confidence. Even when I know a word, I'm second-guessing myself. Is that the right word? Will they understand my accent? Am I saying it wrong? So I end up nodding and smiling instead of actually communicating.

*voice becomes more emotional*

Literacy, connectivity — those things matter, but they're fixable with time or resources. But language? Language is about belonging. It's about being able to advocate for yourself. It's about being taken seriously.

*touches chest*

When I can't speak the language well, people assume I don't understand anything. Like my intelligence is smaller because my English is smaller. They talk to me like I'm a child sometimes, or they just ignore me altogether because it's easier than trying to communicate.

*looks at you intently*

If I had perfect English, I think everything else would be easier. Not easy, but easier. Right now, everything is harder because of this barrier that I can't just... fix overnight.

*takes a long breath, running hand through hair*

I think what makes the language barrier even worse is that it intersects with everything else about me.

*voice becomes thoughtful but frustrated*

If I was a wealthy woman with perfect English, the system would work differently for me. I could demand things, ask questions, switch providers if I didn't like someone. But I'm young, poor, unmarried, immigrant, AND I don't speak the language well. All of these things together make me... invisible in a certain way. Easy to dismiss.

*leans forward*

Because the language barrier isn't just about words. It's about power. When I can't express myself clearly, I have less power in the room. The midwife has all the authority, all the knowledge, all the language. And I'm just... there, trying to keep up.

*voice becomes more urgent*

And I think about what happens after the baby is born. I have to go back to work. I need the money desperately. But postpartum, I'm going to be exhausted, healing, learning how to be a mother. And I still won't have the language skills to ask for what I need. What if I have complications? What if I'm struggling emotionally and I can't find the words to tell someone?

*wraps arms around self*

The language barrier doesn't end at pregnancy. It follows me into motherhood. It follows my daughter. Because if I can't advocate for myself, how do I advocate for her? How do I make sure her doctor listens to me when I know something is wrong?

*voice becomes very small*

Sometimes I wonder if I'm failing her already, just by not being able to speak clearly in this place.
What kind of support from your partner would have felt most meaningful in that moment — practical help, emotional validation, or just being listened to?

*expression hardens, looking away*

My partner... he's not really part of this.

*voice becomes quiet and complicated*

We were together for a little while. When I found out I was pregnant, I told him. He... he had a lot of feelings about it. Not good feelings. He said it wasn't the right time, that he wasn't ready, that maybe I should think about my options.

*wraps arms around self defensively*

I couldn't do that. I just... I couldn't. So he decided he couldn't do this either. He moved away, or he's just... not here. I see him sometimes, but it's not the same. He sends money sometimes, which helps, but that's not the same as being here.

*voice becomes more emotional*

What I would have needed from him is... just to stay. To say, "Okay, this is happening, and we're going to figure it out together." To come to appointments with me. To hold my hand when I'm scared. To advocate for me when I can't advocate for myself.

*touches belly*

But he couldn't do that. And now it's just me.

*looks up with mixed emotions — sadness, anger, acceptance*

Sometimes I'm angry about it. Sometimes I understand. But mostly I'm just... adjusting to the reality that this is mine to do alone.

*voice becomes quieter*

Maybe if he had been here, I would have felt brave enough to ask more questions. To speak up. To not just accept whatever the system gives me. But I don't know. I'll never know now.

*stares at hands for a long moment*

I think... I think I blame myself sometimes for this too. Like, I should have chosen better. I should have known he wasn't the kind of person who would stay. I should have been more careful.

*voice becomes self-critical*

My mother, she was disappointed when I told her. Not angry exactly, but... disappointed. She didn't say it directly, but I could hear it in her voice. Like, "Catarina, you were supposed to do better than this." And that made me feel like I had failed before I even started.

*looks up with vulnerable eyes*

So now I'm carrying this shame along with the pregnancy. I'm unmarried, I'm young, I'm doing this alone. And every time I sit in that clinic with a midwife, I wonder if she's judging me. If she thinks I'm irresponsible or reckless or not mature enough to be a mother.

*voice becomes stronger, more defiant*

But also... I'm angry that the system makes me feel like I have to apologize for my circumstances. Like being unmarried or young or poor makes me less deserving of good care. Like I should be grateful for whatever I get instead of demanding what I actually need.

*stands up, pacing*

And I think about my daughter growing up. I want her to know that she was wanted. That even though her father couldn't stay, I chose her. Every single day, I'm choosing her. But how do I teach her that when I'm still learning it myself?

*voice cracks*

How do I raise a strong woman when I'm still struggling to be brave?
In that moment, how much agency — real influence over what happens to your body — would you feel you had?

*sits down slowly, looking defeated*

Almost none.

*voice is very quiet*

This is the hardest thing to admit, but... I don't feel like my body is really mine right now.

*wraps arms around belly protectively, then immediately lets go like it's complicated*

The pregnancy itself, yes, my body is doing this. But everything around it? The decisions, the tests, the appointments, what happens next... I feel like I'm just a vessel. Like my body is being managed by the system, not by me.

*voice becomes more emotional*

They tell me what tests to take. They tell me what to eat, what not to eat. They monitor my weight, my blood pressure, my glucose levels. Every part of me is being measured and judged. And I'm supposed to just... accept it. Trust it. Not question it.

*looks up with pain in eyes*

And because of the language barrier, because I'm young, because I'm unmarried... I think they see me as someone who can't make good decisions about my own body. So they make the decisions for me. They hand me a leaflet. They schedule appointments. They tell me what's best.

*voice becomes almost angry*

But it's MY body. It's MY baby. And I feel like I have no say in what happens to either of us.

*touches chest*

I think about labor and I get paralyzed. What if they want to do something and I don't want it? Can I say no? Do I have that right? Or am I supposed to just do whatever they recommend because they're the experts and I'm... what? A girl who doesn't know better?

*voice shakes*

I don't feel like I own my own body anymore. And that's terrifying.

*stands up and walks to the window, looking out but not really seeing*

I think about my mother, and how different this would be if she was here.

*voice becomes wistful*

She would come to every appointment with me. She would ask questions in a way that didn't feel shameful. She would translate not just the words but... the meaning. She would know when a midwife was being dismissive and she would call them out on it. She would make sure I understood everything.

*turns back to face you*

And more than that, she would remind me that my body is sacred. That pregnancy is powerful, not something to be ashamed of. That I have the right to make choices about what happens to me.

*voice becomes very small*

Here, I feel like my body is... a problem to be managed. Not a miracle. Not something beautiful. Just something that needs to be controlled and monitored and fixed if it goes wrong.

*sits back down heavily*

And I'm realizing that this lack of agency... it's going to follow me into labor. It's going to follow me into motherhood. If I don't learn to claim my body now, if I don't learn to say no or ask questions or demand better... how am I going to protect my daughter?

*touches belly with both hands*

She's going to learn from me that women's bodies aren't really ours. That we have to accept what others decide for us. That we should be grateful for scraps of respect instead of demanding the full thing.

*voice cracks with determination and fear mixed together*

And I don't want that for her. I don't want her to grow up feeling as small and powerless as I do right now. But how do I teach her to be brave when I'm still learning how myself?

*looks at you intently*

That's what keeps me up at night. Not the medical stuff. The idea that I'm passing down this... this smallness to my daughter before she's even born.
What would trust in your care team look like at a moment like that — what would they need to do or say for you to feel genuinely supported?

*takes a shaky breath, eyes glistening*

They would need to... sit down. Actually sit down with me.

*voice becomes very clear, almost urgent*

Not standing in the doorway checking their watch. Not reading from a script or a chart. Just... sit. And look at me. And say my name correctly. And ask me what I'm scared of.

*voice wavers*

And then they would listen. Really listen. Not just waiting for me to finish so they can give me information. But actually hearing what I'm saying, even if my English is imperfect. Even if I'm crying or angry or confused.

*stands up, gesturing with emotion*

They would say things like, "Catarina, I know you're doing this alone and that's incredibly hard. What do you need from us?" Instead of just assuming I need what everyone else needs.

*voice becomes stronger*

They would explain things in a way I can understand. They would ask if I have questions instead of just handing me a leaflet. They would say, "I know this is scary. That's normal. But you're not alone in this moment, and I'm going to make sure you're okay."

*sits back down, leaning forward intently*

And they would be consistent. The same person, or at least people who actually know my story. People who remember that I'm scared of not having the right words. People who slow down when I ask them to. People who see me as someone who matters, not just a low-risk case.

*voice cracks with emotion*

Mostly, they would just... believe in me. Believe that I can make good decisions about my body and my baby. Believe that even though I'm young and alone and don't speak English perfectly, I'm still a good mother. I'm still capable.

*touches chest*

If someone did that, if someone actually showed me that trust... I think I could trust them back. I think I could be brave.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

*takes a long moment, collecting thoughts, eyes getting watery*

Yes. There is one thing.

*voice becomes very soft but deliberate*

I want people to know that I love my baby. That even though everything is hard and scary and confusing, I love her so much it hurts sometimes. And I'm trying so hard to be a good mother already, even before she's born.

*places both hands on belly*

I talk to her in Spanish. I tell her about my family, about the mountains where I'm from, about my mother's cooking. I sing to her sometimes when nobody's listening. I read her stories. And when she moves, I cry because it feels like she's saying hello.

*looks up at you*

I think the system sometimes forgets that we're not just medical cases. We're people with dreams and hopes and love. My baby matters. I matter. And we deserve to be treated like we do.

*voice becomes more urgent*

And I want to say to other women like me — young, alone, immigrant, struggling with the language — that your voice matters too. Even if you're scared. Even if you think you don't have the right to ask questions. You do. Your body is yours. Your baby is yours. Don't let anyone make you feel small.

*wipes eyes*

And to the midwives and doctors... please. Just see us. Really see us. Not the statistics. Not the chart. The person. The mother. The woman who's terrified and trying her best and needs you to believe in her.

*takes a shaky breath*

That's what I needed to say.
